news_summary: Today's human rights advancements emphasize improved economic measurement through science, landmark justice efforts in Libya, enhanced protection for vulnerable adults, and expanded labor rights for gig workers.
news_summary_fr: Les avancées actuelles en matière de droits de l'homme mettent l'accent sur l'amélioration des indicateurs économiques grâce à la science, sur des initiatives judiciaires historiques en Libye, sur le renforcement de la protection des adultes vulnérables et sur l'élargissement des droits du travail pour les travailleurs de la gig economy.
news_summary_es: Los avances de hoy en materia de derechos humanos se centran en la mejora de los indicadores económicos gracias a la ciencia, en iniciativas judiciales históricas en Libia, en una mayor protección de los adultos vulnerables y en la ampliación de los derechos laborales de los trabajadores de la economía colaborativa.

articles.title: Science can take the lead in making better measures of economic growth
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 12 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01474-zThe United Nations wants scientists to help design indicators of progress that go beyond GDP. Researchers should seize the chance and be aware of past failures.
articles.content:
The United Nations wants scientists to help design indicators of progress that go beyond GDP. Researchers should seize the chance and be aware of past failures.

Harmful impacts of greenhouse-gas emissions, such as those from transport, are not accounted for in GDP.Credit: RJ Sangosti/The Denver Post/Getty

“Growth at any cost leaves us all poorer.” Those were the words of United Nations secretary-general António Guterres last week at the launch of a landmark report, Counting What Counts, which he commissioned from a team of researchers and policymakers (www.un.org/beyondgdp). It proposes how countries can move beyond gross domestic product (GDP), the world’s main indicator for the health of economies.

How to measure a good life — tips for moving beyond GDP

GDP has its roots in a concept proposed in the 1930s (see go.nature.com/4324jwf), and GDP growth has since become the main economic-policy objective for most governments. Drops in GDP are often seen by markets, the media and commentators as a sign of government incompetence. However, this kind of growth has coincided with persistent inequality and environmental degradation worldwide, as the report says. If economic development is to benefit as many people as possible and to be sustainable in the long term, a measure is needed that captures these factors — and GDP does not.

The authors have compiled 31 indicators, covering human rights, peace and respect for the planet, that they recommend governments measure (see Nature https://doi.org/q57n; 2026). Of these, 15 are already indicators for the UN Sustainable Development Goals. The report also recommends that a committee of scientists be appointed to work on one or more headline indicators that could aggregate variables into one quantity, much as GDP does. The governments of Spain and Guyana have been handed the baton to take the recommendations forwards.

Goodbye GDP? 31 ways to replace the world’s favourite measure of economic health

The invitation of scientists to participate in the process is a welcome development, because until now the theory and application of GDP has mostly been the preserve of economists and economic statisticians. Any effort to distil the 31 indicators into a few headline statistics will need input from many disciplines. All who are involved in the next phase must study previous efforts to complement GDP and learn from past successes and failures. This is a once-in-a-generation opportunity to correct a long-standing flaw.

By a common method of calculation, GDP is the sum of the value of goods and services; what households and governments spend added to investment from businesses; and income. As the report acknowledges, one reason for the measure’s enduring attractiveness is that it is easy to understand and communicate, and enables comparisons to be made over time and between countries and regions. Simplicity was also a hallmark of the Human Development Index (HDI), which was created in a previous attempt to dethrone GDP more than three decades ago.

When the Pakistani economist Mahbub ul Haq began to plan the HDI with his friend, the Indian economist Amartya Sen, he said he wanted to create an index that would be like GDP “but would stand for better things”. The HDI architects chose three measures: income, education and life expectancy at birth. They assigned an equal weight to each and then converted countries’ performance into a single, comparable number (A. D. Sagar and A. Najam Ecol. Econ. 25, 249–264; 1998).

To move beyond GDP, don’t ignore ecological economics

The hard part for those involved in the latest attempt to move beyond GDP will be to choose which of the 31 indicators to focus on. They must also decide whether those should be given equal weighting or whether they should prioritize some variables over others, and whether (as with GDP) the indicator should have a monetary value. These obstacles can be overcome: existing headline indicators constructed from different variables include gross ecosystem product, which quantifies the value of nature to economies, and the multidimensional poverty index, an estimate of how many people face deprivation (measured by indicators such as housing, child mortality and availability of clean water).

But the use of these indicators hasn’t changed most governments’ focus on maximizing GDP. One reason is that GDP has its own rules, called the System of National Accounts, which are set by the UN Statistical Commission (UNSC), a decision-making body comprising the chiefs of national statistics offices. This group is extraordinarily influential, but historically it is not used to engaging with other organizations, even within the UN system. If a new indicator is to have at least the same importance as GDP, then the UNSC must put its weight behind it.

It is good to see that Guterres, UN member states and the report’s authors underscore the necessity of working with the UNSC and with many other stakeholders. In championing the search for alternatives to GDP, Guterres has chosen what can be called the hard problem of economic policy. Hard, but not impossible. The authors of Counting What Counts have laid strong foundations. Let’s get the world’s best brains on the case to design something with similarly lasting influence.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant and inspiring initiative by the United Nations to develop new, comprehensive indicators of economic progress beyond GDP that incorporate human rights, environmental sustainability, and social well-being. This effort addresses a major global issue—how to measure true societal progress in a way that benefits broad populations and the planet. The involvement of scientists and policymakers, the detailed proposal of 31 indicators, and the commitment from multiple countries highlight the broad scope, positive real-world impact, and substantive nature of the story.
articles.personality_title: United Nations pushes for new measures beyond GDP to track real progress
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – For nearly a century, countries have used Gross Domestic Product (GDP) to measure how well their economies are doing. GDP adds up the value of all goods and services produced, but it ignores important things like the environment, human rights, and social fairness.

**What happened** – The United Nations recently released a report called "Counting What Counts," urging scientists and governments to create better ways to measure progress. This report suggests 31 new indicators that include things like peace, respect for the planet, and human rights. Spain and Guyana are leading the effort to develop these new measures.

**Impact** – This is important because GDP growth alone doesn’t show if people’s lives are improving or if the environment is safe. By including more factors, countries can make decisions that help more people and protect nature. Involving scientists from different fields aims to create clear and useful measures that everyone can understand, similar to how GDP works today.

**What's next step** – The next challenge is to decide which indicators to focus on and how to combine them into one or a few clear numbers. The United Nations Statistical Commission, which sets the current rules for GDP, will need to support these new measures for them to become widely accepted. This effort could change how governments plan their economies in the future.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The United Nations is leading a global effort to create new, broader measures of progress that go beyond GDP to better reflect human well-being and environmental health.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les Nations Unies encouragent de nouveaux indicateurs pour mesurer le vrai progrès au-delà du PIB
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Depuis près d’un siècle, les pays utilisent le Produit Intérieur Brut (PIB) pour évaluer la santé de leur économie. Le PIB additionne la valeur de tous les biens et services produits, mais il ignore des aspects essentiels comme l’environnement, les droits humains et la justice sociale.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Les Nations Unies ont publié un rapport intitulé « Compter ce qui compte », appelant scientifiques et gouvernements à créer de meilleures façons de mesurer le progrès. Ce rapport propose 31 nouveaux indicateurs couvrant la paix, le respect de la planète et les droits humains. L’Espagne et la Guyane pilotent le développement de ces mesures.

**Impact** – Cela est important car la croissance du PIB seule ne montre pas si la vie des gens s’améliore ou si l’environnement est préservé. En intégrant plus de facteurs, les pays pourront prendre des décisions qui bénéficient à davantage de personnes et protègent la nature. L’implication de scientifiques de divers domaines vise à créer des mesures claires et utiles, similaires au PIB aujourd’hui.

**Prochaines étapes** – Le défi suivant est de choisir quels indicateurs privilégier et comment les combiner en un ou quelques chiffres simples. La Commission statistique des Nations Unies, qui établit les règles actuelles du PIB, devra soutenir ces nouvelles mesures pour qu’elles soient largement adoptées. Cette initiative pourrait transformer la manière dont les gouvernements planifient leur économie.

**Phrase clé** – Les Nations Unies mènent un effort mondial pour créer de nouveaux indicateurs plus larges que le PIB, reflétant mieux le bien-être humain et la santé de l’environnement.
articles.personality_title_es: Naciones Unidas impulsa nuevas medidas para evaluar el progreso real más allá del PIB
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Durante casi un siglo, los países han usado el Producto Interno Bruto (PIB) para medir el desempeño de sus economías. El PIB suma el valor de todos los bienes y servicios producidos, pero no toma en cuenta aspectos importantes como el medio ambiente, los derechos humanos y la justicia social.

**Qué pasó** – Las Naciones Unidas publicaron un informe llamado "Contar lo que importa", que invita a científicos y gobiernos a crear mejores formas de medir el progreso. Este informe propone 31 nuevos indicadores que incluyen la paz, el respeto al planeta y los derechos humanos. España y Guyana están liderando el desarrollo de estas nuevas medidas.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque el crecimiento del PIB por sí solo no muestra si la vida de las personas mejora o si el medio ambiente está protegido. Al incluir más factores, los países podrán tomar decisiones que beneficien a más personas y cuiden la naturaleza. La participación de científicos de distintas áreas busca crear medidas claras y útiles, similares al PIB actual.

**Próximo paso** – El siguiente reto es decidir qué indicadores priorizar y cómo combinarlos en uno o pocos números claros. La Comisión Estadística de las Naciones Unidas, que establece las reglas del PIB, deberá apoyar estas nuevas medidas para que sean aceptadas globalmente. Esto podría cambiar la forma en que los gobiernos planifican sus economías.

**Resumen en una frase** – Las Naciones Unidas lideran un esfuerzo global para crear medidas más amplias que el PIB, que reflejen mejor el bienestar humano y la salud del medio ambiente.
articles.image_prompt: A harmonious, detailed painting of a balanced scale with one side holding a glowing, intricate globe representing the planet’s environment and human well-being, and the other side holding a traditional economic chart subtly fading away; the background softly blends natural earth tones with gentle abstract patterns symbolizing diverse indicators of progress beyond GDP.

articles.title: ICC: Landmark First Hearing in Libya Atrocity Case
articles.summary:
Click to expand Image
          



  
        
  

        
                      
      
        
              Khaled Mohamed Ali El Hishri appeared before Pre-Trial Chamber I of the International Criminal Court in The Hague, Netherlands, December 3, 2025.
                    © 2025 International Criminal Court 
          
    


  
(The Hague) – The International Criminal Court’s (ICC) upcoming landmark hearing in the case of Khaled Mohamed Ali El Hishri, suspected of crimes against humanity and war crimes, is a long-awaited breakthrough for victims of serious crimes in Libya, Human Rights Watch said today. From May 19 to 21, 2026, ICC judges will hear evidence against El Hishri in a “confirmation of charges” hearing to determine whether the case against him should proceed to trial. He is the first person to face justice before the ICC concerning atrocities in Libya since the United Nations Security Council referred the situation in Libya to the ICC prosecutor in 2011, to investigate serious crimes committed following the country’s uprising. Human Rights Watch has published a question-and-answer document about the upcoming proceedings.“Finally seeing a suspect on the docket at the ICC, 15 years after the end of Libya’s 2011 revolution, sends a powerful message to thousands of victims of serious crimes in Libya that their struggle for justice has not been forgotten,” said Alice Autin, international justice researcher at Human Rights Watch. “As atrocities persist across Libya, progress in this case should spur action from Libyan authorities and the international community to end the pervasive impunity that continues to fuel violence.”El Hishri is a former senior member of the Deterrence Apparatus for Countering Terrorism and Organized Crime (al-Radaa), a Tripoli-based militia affiliated with the Presidential Council and formerly known as the Special Deterrence Force. The ICC Office of the Prosecutor alleges that El Hishri is responsible for 17 counts of war crimes and crimes against humanity including torture, rape, sexual violence, murder, enslavement, and persecution. The charges relate to crimes allegedly committed at Libya’s notorious Mitiga Prison in Tripoli, between 2014 and 2020, against both Libyan and non-Libyan detainees. El Hishri is alleged to have directly committed, ordered, and facilitated these crimes through the authority he wielded over the prison.German authorities arrested El Hishri in July 2025 on an ICC warrant for alleged war crimes and crimes against humanity committed in Mitiga Prison. They surrendered him to the court, in The Hague, in December 2025. Human Rights Watch, other human rights and humanitarian organizations as well as the UN have documented inhumane conditions in detention centers and prisons across Libya, many run by abusive and unaccountable armed groups nominally affiliated with the authorities. Successive Libyan governments and interim authorities have failed to end this practice or to investigate and hold accountable those responsible for serious abuses committed in the detention centers.The ICC has issued public arrest warrants against 14 individuals in relation to the Libya investigation, including El Hishri. Four have since died or were killed, and eight others remain at large. ICC judges declared the case against Abdullah al-Senussi, the intelligence chief under former Libyan leader Muammar Gaddafi, inadmissible before the court. German authorities’ successful arrest and surrender of El Hishri to the ICC is especially important because it demonstrates how ICC member countries can contribute to the court’s delivery of justice when they fulfill their obligation to cooperate with the ICC, Human Rights Watch said.In January 2025, Italy, also an ICC member, failed to surrender El Hishri’s alleged co-perpetrator at Mitiga Prison, Osama Elmasry Njeem, to the court after arresting him. Instead, Italian authorities sent him back to Libya. In October 2025, ICC judges found that Italy breached its obligation to cooperate with the court and, in January 2026, referred it to the court’s member countries for further action.Under the Security Council resolution that referred the situation in Libya to the ICC prosecutor and Libyan authorities’ 2025 decision to accept the court’s jurisdiction from 2011 to 2027, Libya—though not an ICC member—has a clear obligation to cooperate with the court. This includes the arrest and transfer to the ICC of individuals wanted by the court who are on its territory. However, Libya’s cooperation with the court to date has remained largely inadequate. Some Libyan authorities have opposed the trial of Libyans outside of Libya as a matter of principle and questioned the need for the ICC’s involvement in some investigations in the country. Libyan authorities have reportedly arrested at least two suspects also wanted by the ICC in relations to serious crimes in Tarhuna, based on domestic investigations. They also reportedly arrested Njeem in Tripoli in November 2025 and placed him in pretrial detention.The ICC is a court of last resort and national courts have the primary responsibility to investigate and prosecute serious crimes committed on their territory. However, when ICC investigations have already led to arrest warrants, domestic authorities have to demonstrate to the court that they are trying the ICC suspects for the same crimes being tried by the court. Njeem’s defense counsel has filed a challenge with ICC judges, claiming that there are ongoing criminal proceedings against Njeem in Libya that cover substantially the same conduct for which he is wanted by the ICC. The issue is currently pending before the ICC judges. Libyan authorities should swiftly transfer all ICC suspects in their custody to the court in The Hague, Human Rights Watch said.Human Rights Watch has found that Libya’s fragmented justice sector remains marred by serious due process violations and laws that breach international norms, and that the judiciary is unwilling and unable to meaningfully investigate serious crimes.“The El Hishri confirmation of charges hearing highlights how important it is for countries to cooperate with the ICC,” Autin said. “Further progress on justice for serious crimes in Libya hinges on Libyan authorities fulfilling their legal duty to surrender suspects sought by the ICC.”
articles.content:
Click to expand Image Khaled Mohamed Ali El Hishri appeared before Pre-Trial Chamber I of the International Criminal Court in The Hague, Netherlands, December 3, 2025. © 2025 International Criminal Court

(The Hague) – The International Criminal Court’s (ICC) upcoming landmark hearing in the case of Khaled Mohamed Ali El Hishri, suspected of crimes against humanity and war crimes, is a long-awaited breakthrough for victims of serious crimes in Libya, Human Rights Watch said today.

From May 19 to 21, 2026, ICC judges will hear evidence against El Hishri in a “confirmation of charges” hearing to determine whether the case against him should proceed to trial. He is the first person to face justice before the ICC concerning atrocities in Libya since the United Nations Security Council referred the situation in Libya to the ICC prosecutor in 2011, to investigate serious crimes committed following the country’s uprising. Human Rights Watch has published a question-and-answer document about the upcoming proceedings.

“Finally seeing a suspect on the docket at the ICC, 15 years after the end of Libya’s 2011 revolution, sends a powerful message to thousands of victims of serious crimes in Libya that their struggle for justice has not been forgotten,” said Alice Autin, international justice researcher at Human Rights Watch. “As atrocities persist across Libya, progress in this case should spur action from Libyan authorities and the international community to end the pervasive impunity that continues to fuel violence.”

El Hishri is a former senior member of the Deterrence Apparatus for Countering Terrorism and Organized Crime (al-Radaa), a Tripoli-based militia affiliated with the Presidential Council and formerly known as the Special Deterrence Force.

The ICC Office of the Prosecutor alleges that El Hishri is responsible for 17 counts of war crimes and crimes against humanity including torture, rape, sexual violence, murder, enslavement, and persecution. The charges relate to crimes allegedly committed at Libya’s notorious Mitiga Prison in Tripoli, between 2014 and 2020, against both Libyan and non-Libyan detainees. El Hishri is alleged to have directly committed, ordered, and facilitated these crimes through the authority he wielded over the prison.

German authorities arrested El Hishri in July 2025 on an ICC warrant for alleged war crimes and crimes against humanity committed in Mitiga Prison. They surrendered him to the court, in The Hague, in December 2025.

Human Rights Watch, other human rights and humanitarian organizations as well as the UN have documented inhumane conditions in detention centers and prisons across Libya, many run by abusive and unaccountable armed groups nominally affiliated with the authorities. Successive Libyan governments and interim authorities have failed to end this practice or to investigate and hold accountable those responsible for serious abuses committed in the detention centers.

The ICC has issued public arrest warrants against 14 individuals in relation to the Libya investigation, including El Hishri. Four have since died or were killed, and eight others remain at large. ICC judges declared the case against Abdullah al-Senussi, the intelligence chief under former Libyan leader Muammar Gaddafi, inadmissible before the court.

German authorities’ successful arrest and surrender of El Hishri to the ICC is especially important because it demonstrates how ICC member countries can contribute to the court’s delivery of justice when they fulfill their obligation to cooperate with the ICC, Human Rights Watch said.

In January 2025, Italy, also an ICC member, failed to surrender El Hishri’s alleged co-perpetrator at Mitiga Prison, Osama Elmasry Njeem, to the court after arresting him. Instead, Italian authorities sent him back to Libya. In October 2025, ICC judges found that Italy breached its obligation to cooperate with the court and, in January 2026, referred it to the court’s member countries for further action.

Under the Security Council resolution that referred the situation in Libya to the ICC prosecutor and Libyan authorities’ 2025 decision to accept the court’s jurisdiction from 2011 to 2027, Libya—though not an ICC member—has a clear obligation to cooperate with the court. This includes the arrest and transfer to the ICC of individuals wanted by the court who are on its territory. However, Libya’s cooperation with the court to date has remained largely inadequate. Some Libyan authorities have opposed the trial of Libyans outside of Libya as a matter of principle and questioned the need for the ICC’s involvement in some investigations in the country.

Libyan authorities have reportedly arrested at least two suspects also wanted by the ICC in relations to serious crimes in Tarhuna, based on domestic investigations. They also reportedly arrested Njeem in Tripoli in November 2025 and placed him in pretrial detention.

The ICC is a court of last resort and national courts have the primary responsibility to investigate and prosecute serious crimes committed on their territory. However, when ICC investigations have already led to arrest warrants, domestic authorities have to demonstrate to the court that they are trying the ICC suspects for the same crimes being tried by the court. Njeem’s defense counsel has filed a challenge with ICC judges, claiming that there are ongoing criminal proceedings against Njeem in Libya that cover substantially the same conduct for which he is wanted by the ICC. The issue is currently pending before the ICC judges. Libyan authorities should swiftly transfer all ICC suspects in their custody to the court in The Hague, Human Rights Watch said.

Human Rights Watch has found that Libya’s fragmented justice sector remains marred by serious due process violations and laws that breach international norms, and that the judiciary is unwilling and unable to meaningfully investigate serious crimes.

“The El Hishri confirmation of charges hearing highlights how important it is for countries to cooperate with the ICC,” Autin said. “Further progress on justice for serious crimes in Libya hinges on Libyan authorities fulfilling their legal duty to surrender suspects sought by the ICC.”
articles.reasoning: The article reports a landmark ICC hearing against a suspect accused of serious war crimes and crimes against humanity in Libya, marking a significant step toward justice for victims of atrocities. This event has broad significance for international justice and human rights, demonstrating progress in accountability and the potential to reduce impunity for grave crimes in Libya. The article provides detailed context on the case, the charges, and the international cooperation involved, fulfilling criteria for substance and positive real-world impact.
articles.personality_title: International Criminal Court holds first hearing in Libya atrocity case
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Since 2011, serious crimes have been reported in Libya after the country’s uprising. The United Nations Security Council asked the International Criminal Court (ICC) to investigate these crimes. Until now, no one has faced trial at the ICC for these events.

**What happened** – In December 2025, Khaled Mohamed Ali El Hishri was arrested in Germany and sent to the ICC in The Hague. He is accused of war crimes and crimes against humanity, including torture and murder, at Libya’s Mitiga Prison between 2014 and 2020. From May 19 to 21, 2026, ICC judges will hold a hearing to decide if the case against him should go to trial. This is the first time someone linked to Libya’s atrocities has appeared before the ICC.

**Impact** – This hearing is important because it shows that justice can reach those responsible for serious crimes in Libya, even many years after the events. It sends a message to victims that their suffering is being taken seriously. Also, it highlights how countries like Germany can help by arresting suspects and cooperating with international courts. The case may encourage Libyan authorities and others to take stronger action against impunity.

**What’s next step** – The ICC judges will decide if El Hishri’s case will proceed to trial. Meanwhile, Libya and other countries are expected to cooperate by arresting and handing over other suspects wanted by the ICC. The court will also review challenges about whether Libya is trying some suspects fairly at home. The outcome could influence how justice is handled for other war crimes in Libya.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The ICC’s upcoming hearing on El Hishri marks a key moment in seeking justice for serious crimes committed in Libya since 2011.
articles.personality_title_fr: La Cour pénale internationale tient la première audience dans une affaire d’atrocités en Libye
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Depuis 2011, des crimes graves ont été signalés en Libye après le soulèvement du pays. Le Conseil de sécurité des Nations Unies a demandé à la Cour pénale internationale (CPI) d’enquêter sur ces crimes. Jusqu’à présent, personne n’a été jugé par la CPI pour ces événements.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En décembre 2025, Khaled Mohamed Ali El Hishri a été arrêté en Allemagne puis transféré à la CPI à La Haye. Il est accusé de crimes de guerre et de crimes contre l’humanité, dont la torture et le meurtre, dans la prison de Mitiga en Libye entre 2014 et 2020. Du 19 au 21 mai 2026, des juges de la CPI tiendront une audience pour décider si l’affaire doit aller en procès. C’est la première fois qu’une personne liée aux atrocités en Libye comparaît devant la CPI.

**Impact** – Cette audience est importante car elle montre que la justice peut atteindre les responsables de crimes graves en Libye, même longtemps après les faits. Cela envoie un message aux victimes que leurs souffrances sont prises au sérieux. Elle montre aussi comment des pays comme l’Allemagne peuvent aider en arrêtant les suspects et en coopérant avec les tribunaux internationaux. Ce cas pourrait encourager les autorités libyennes et d’autres à agir contre l’impunité.

**Prochaine étape** – Les juges de la CPI décideront si l’affaire d’El Hishri ira en procès. Pendant ce temps, la Libye et d’autres pays doivent coopérer en arrêtant et en remettant d’autres suspects recherchés par la CPI. La cour examinera aussi les contestations concernant les procès en Libye. Le résultat pourrait influencer la manière dont la justice est rendue pour d’autres crimes en Libye.

**Résumé en une phrase** – L’audience prochaine à la CPI sur El Hishri marque un moment clé dans la recherche de justice pour les crimes graves commis en Libye depuis 2011.
articles.personality_title_es: La Corte Penal Internacional celebra la primera audiencia en un caso de atrocidades en Libia
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Desde 2011, se han reportado crímenes graves en Libia tras el levantamiento del país. El Consejo de Seguridad de las Naciones Unidas pidió a la Corte Penal Internacional (CPI) que investigara estos crímenes. Hasta ahora, nadie había sido juzgado por la CPI por estos hechos.

**Qué pasó** – En diciembre de 2025, Khaled Mohamed Ali El Hishri fue arrestado en Alemania y enviado a la CPI en La Haya. Está acusado de crímenes de guerra y crímenes contra la humanidad, incluyendo tortura y asesinato, en la prisión de Mitiga en Libia entre 2014 y 2020. Del 19 al 21 de mayo de 2026, los jueces de la CPI celebrarán una audiencia para decidir si el caso debe ir a juicio. Es la primera vez que alguien vinculado a las atrocidades en Libia comparece ante la CPI.

**Impacto** – Esta audiencia es importante porque muestra que la justicia puede alcanzar a los responsables de crímenes graves en Libia, incluso muchos años después de los hechos. Envía un mensaje a las víctimas de que su sufrimiento está siendo tomado en serio. También destaca cómo países como Alemania pueden ayudar arrestando sospechosos y cooperando con tribunales internacionales. El caso puede motivar a las autoridades libias y a otros a tomar medidas contra la impunidad.

**Próximo paso** – Los jueces de la CPI decidirán si el caso de El Hishri seguirá a juicio. Mientras tanto, Libia y otros países deben cooperar arrestando y entregando a otros sospechosos buscados por la CPI. La corte también revisará desafíos sobre si Libia está juzgando justamente a algunos sospechosos en su territorio. El resultado podría influir en cómo se maneja la justicia para otros crímenes en Libia.

**Resumen en una frase** – La próxima audiencia de la CPI sobre El Hishri marca un momento clave en la búsqueda de justicia por crímenes graves cometidos en Libia desde 2011.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of a balanced set of old-fashioned scales held by a pair of strong hands emerging from a calm sea, one scale containing a stylized prison tower with barred windows symbolizing Mitiga Prison, the other scale holding a glowing olive branch representing justice and hope, all rendered in warm, natural earth tones and soft blues under a serene sky.

articles.title: Press release - Deal to improve the protection of vulnerable adults
articles.summary: The new legislation agreed on Tuesday seeks to foster the right to autonomy for adults across the EU.Committee on Legal Affairs Source : © European Union, 2026 - EP
articles.content:
Parliament and EU government negotiators reached a provisional agreement on new rules to ensure that adults in need of assistance in matters such as medical care, their place of residence, real estate, trade or the management of property and assets will receive more support and better protection in cross-border cases. The legislation does not apply to cross-border cases related to marriage, succession, social security, or maintenance obligations.

Respecting a person’s choice

The new rules will allow adults to organise their own protection and support in anticipation of a time when they would not be able to look after these issues themselves. The rules will determine which legal frameworks will deal with cross-border matters, while ensuring the person’s choices are respected. Measures taken in one EU country should not require any special procedure to be recognised in another, although EU countries will be able to refuse to recognise a measure taken in another member state if the adult was not given a genuine and effective hearing.

MEPs successfully pushed for adults to be provided with appropriate support and access to relevant information by EU countries, including through a practice guide on the application of the regulation and factsheets with a short summary of national legislation.

European Certificate of Support and Representation

According to the agreed text, adults in need of support will be able to request a European certificate of support and representation for use across the EU.

The certificate will also be able to be used to demonstrate that a designated person is authorised to represent or support the adult. MEPs ensured the certificate will be issued free of charge or for a fee not exceeding the administrative costs of its production, and where possible in a digital format.To ensure privacy, negotiators agreed to delete interconnected registers from the legislation but strengthened electronic communication between authorities and with citizens.

Quote

Rapporteur Yana Toom (Renew, EE) said: “We are making life easier for adults that need protection or support in another country. With the new rules, the Parliament aimed for legal certainty and ensuring the highest respect for human rights, in particular the right to equal recognition before the law.”

Next steps

Once formally approved by the Parliament as a whole and the Council, the regulation will enter into force twenty days after its publication in the Official Journal.

Background

The proposal for this regulation builds on the rules of the International Protection of Adults Convention adopted by the Hague Conference on Private International Law (HCCH 2 000). It aims to bridge existing gaps in governing judicial cooperation in the field of the protection of adults across EU countries.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant legislative agreement within the EU to improve legal protection and support for vulnerable adults across borders, ensuring their autonomy and rights are respected. This has broad societal impact across EU member states, provides clear context and details about the new rules, and represents a meaningful advancement in human rights protections.
articles.personality_title: EU agrees new rules to protect vulnerable adults across borders
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The European Union is working to better protect adults who need help managing important matters like medical care or property, especially when these issues involve more than one EU country.

**What happened** – On Tuesday, EU Parliament and government negotiators reached an agreement on new rules. These rules will help adults organize their own support before they might lose the ability to make decisions. They also set clear legal guidelines for handling cases that cross borders within the EU. For example, if someone needs help managing their home or finances in a different EU country, these rules will make sure their choices are respected and their rights protected. The agreement also introduces a European certificate of support that shows who is authorized to help the adult, and it will be available free or at a low cost.

**Impact** – This agreement is important because it makes it easier and safer for vulnerable adults to get help across different countries without complicated legal problems. It respects their right to choose who supports them and ensures that decisions made in one country are recognized in others. This reduces confusion and delays, which can be very stressful for adults who need assistance. For example, if an adult living in France needs support from a family member in Germany, this new law will make the process smoother.

**What's next step** – The new rules still need to be formally approved by the full Parliament and the EU Council. After that, the regulation will become law twenty days after it is published officially. EU countries will then begin using the new system to protect vulnerable adults.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The EU has agreed on new laws that improve support and legal protection for vulnerable adults across member countries, making it easier for their rights and choices to be respected across borders.
articles.personality_title_fr: L'UE adopte de nouvelles règles pour protéger les adultes vulnérables à travers les frontières
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – L'Union européenne souhaite mieux protéger les adultes qui ont besoin d'aide pour gérer des questions importantes comme les soins médicaux ou les biens, surtout lorsque ces questions concernent plusieurs pays de l'UE.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Mardi, le Parlement européen et les négociateurs gouvernementaux ont trouvé un accord sur de nouvelles règles. Celles-ci permettront aux adultes d'organiser leur propre soutien avant de perdre la capacité de prendre des décisions. Elles fixent aussi des règles claires pour gérer les cas qui dépassent les frontières au sein de l'UE. Par exemple, si quelqu'un a besoin d'aide pour gérer sa maison ou ses finances dans un autre pays de l'UE, ces règles garantiront le respect de ses choix et la protection de ses droits. L'accord introduit aussi un certificat européen de soutien qui montre qui est autorisé à aider l'adulte, disponible gratuitement ou à faible coût.

**Impact** – Cet accord est important car il facilite et sécurise l'aide aux adultes vulnérables entre différents pays sans complications juridiques. Il respecte leur droit de choisir leur soutien et assure que les décisions prises dans un pays sont reconnues dans les autres. Cela réduit la confusion et les retards, souvent source de stress pour les adultes en besoin d'assistance. Par exemple, si un adulte vivant en France a besoin d'aide d'un membre de sa famille en Allemagne, cette nouvelle loi simplifiera la procédure.

**Prochaine étape** – Ces nouvelles règles doivent encore être approuvées officiellement par le Parlement complet et le Conseil de l'UE. Ensuite, le règlement entrera en vigueur vingt jours après sa publication officielle. Les pays de l'UE commenceront alors à utiliser ce nouveau système pour protéger les adultes vulnérables.

**Message clé en une phrase** – L'UE a adopté de nouvelles lois qui améliorent le soutien et la protection juridique des adultes vulnérables dans les pays membres, facilitant le respect de leurs droits et choix au-delà des frontières.
articles.personality_title_es: La UE acuerda nuevas normas para proteger a adultos vulnerables a través de fronteras
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La Unión Europea quiere proteger mejor a los adultos que necesitan ayuda para manejar asuntos importantes como la atención médica o sus propiedades, especialmente cuando estos temas involucran a más de un país de la UE.

**Qué pasó** – El martes, el Parlamento y negociadores gubernamentales de la UE llegaron a un acuerdo sobre nuevas reglas. Estas reglas permitirán que los adultos organicen su propio apoyo antes de que puedan perder la capacidad de tomar decisiones. También establecen normas claras para manejar casos que cruzan fronteras dentro de la UE. Por ejemplo, si una persona necesita ayuda para administrar su casa o finanzas en otro país de la UE, estas reglas asegurarán que se respeten sus decisiones y derechos. El acuerdo también crea un certificado europeo de apoyo que muestra quién está autorizado para ayudar al adulto, y estará disponible gratis o a bajo costo.

**Impacto** – Este acuerdo es importante porque facilita y asegura que los adultos vulnerables reciban ayuda entre países sin problemas legales complicados. Respeta su derecho a elegir quién los apoya y garantiza que las decisiones tomadas en un país sean reconocidas en otros. Esto reduce la confusión y los retrasos, que pueden ser muy estresantes para quienes necesitan asistencia. Por ejemplo, si un adulto que vive en Francia necesita apoyo de un familiar en Alemania, esta nueva ley hará que el proceso sea más sencillo.

**Próximo paso** – Las nuevas reglas aún deben ser aprobadas formalmente por todo el Parlamento y el Consejo de la UE. Después, el reglamento entrará en vigor veinte días después de su publicación oficial. Los países de la UE comenzarán a usar el nuevo sistema para proteger a los adultos vulnerables.

**Mensaje clave en una frase** – La UE ha acordado nuevas leyes que mejoran el apoyo y la protección legal para adultos vulnerables en los países miembros, facilitando que se respeten sus derechos y decisiones a través de las fronteras.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a sturdy, open hand gently supporting a glowing, translucent house and a set of scales balanced with miniature keys and documents, all interconnected by soft, glowing threads symbolizing cross-border legal protection and personal choice.

articles.title: ILO Labor Treaty Should Protect All Gig Workers
articles.summary:
Click to expand Image
          



  
        
  

        
                      
      
        
              Delivery workers driving through New Delhi, India, 2026.
                    © 2026 Human Rights Watch 
          
    


  
(New York) – Gig workers around the world experience long hours, unpredictable and declining pay, and serious safety risks, Human Rights Watch said in a report released today. Governments negotiating a landmark treaty under the International Labour Organization (ILO) on platform work in June 2026 should adopt strong, binding standards to ensure fair pay, safe working conditions, and access to social security for gig workers worldwide.The multimedia report, “Algorithms of Exploitation: Rights Abuses in the Gig Economy and the Global Fight for Change,” documents the experiences of platform workers across nine countries, including India, Kenya, Kuwait, Lebanon, Mexico, Pakistan, Saudi Arabia, the UAE, and the United Kingdom. Human Rights Watch found that workers across all countries studied face low and unstable earnings, unsafe working conditions, and little or no protection when they are injured or unable to work.  



      

  
    
  


            
        
                                    
      
                    
              
        
      
        
                                     
            Algorithms of Exploitation
      
                                 
              
                                








      
      Rights Abuses in the Gig Economy and the Global Fight For Change
    
      



  
        
  


                
    


  
“Platform companies have built a business model that sidesteps labor protections and shifts risks and costs onto the workers,” said Lena Simet, senior economic justice advisor at Human Rights Watch. “The ILO negotiations are the first global effort to get governments to course correct and ensure that using this model does not come at the expense of workers’ rights.”Human Rights Watch interviewed drivers and delivery workers in India, Kenya, Lebanon, Mexico, Pakistan, and the UK as well as migrant returnees from Bangladesh and Nepal who previously worked for companies in Saudi Arabia, the UAE, and Kuwait to show examples of the human impact of unregulated work in the platform economy.The ILO estimates that platform work nearly doubled between 2016 and 2021, while the World Bank estimates that up to 435 million people worldwide earn income through these labor platforms. Yet labor protections have not kept pace.The convention under negotiation should address existing gaps in protection by including key guarantees for all platform workers, regardless of employment status, Human Rights Watch said. Because platform companies routinely classify workers as independent contractors or self-employed, this in many countries excludes them from minimum wage, social security, and occupational safety guarantees.Workers described long hours, unpredictable and declining pay, and serious safety risks, often without social security or support if an injury or illness left them unable to work.Apraham Orfalian, a driver in Beirut, said his earnings have declined steadily since 2015, leaving him unable to cover everyday expenses or contribute to social security. After his car and phone were stolen in a violent robbery while he was working for Uber, he was left without income and said he received no support from the company. Agnes Mwongera, a driver in Nairobi, said she had been assaulted by a passenger and received no response when she reported the attack to her company.Graeme Franes, a courier who used a bicycle to deliver food in Scotland, UK, said he was unable to work for six months after an attack left him with a broken arm. “I had to rely on friends and family,” he said. “That was a really tough time.”By classifying gig workers as independent contractors, companies in many countries are able to avoid obligations for minimum wages, occupational safety, and social security. At the same time, they exercise significant control over workers through algorithmic systems that determine pay, assign tasks, and can suspend workers often without transparency or effective recourse.Human Rights Watch previously found that after expenses, many platform workers in the United States earn well below a living wage and the statutory minimum wage. Workers in other countries reported similar dynamics, with earnings often insufficient to meet everyday expenses. This exploitative model allows companies to capture a growing share of revenue while shifting costs onto workers, contributing to widening inequality in labor markets.Human Rights Watch urges governments to adopt strong standards that:Establish a presumption of employment for workers when companies exercise control over them, to prevent misclassification; Require fair pay, including compensation for all working time, and earnings that meet at least minimum wage or living wage standards; Guarantee access to social security for all workers, including in cases of injury, illness, unemployment, and older age; Mandate algorithmic transparency from platform companies, including information for workers on how their pay is calculated, how tasks and jobs are priced and assigned, and how incentive programs work; Ensure accountability for platform companies, including accessible ways for workers to challenge automated decisions, including decisions to deactivate their accounts;Extend occupational health and safety protections to all platform workers and require the companies to adopt safeguards against extreme heat and other dangerous working conditions; andGuarantee workers’ rights to organize and bargain collectively without retaliation. Human Rights Watch has contributed to the ILO process through submissions outlining rights-aligned approaches to regulating platform work, including proposals developed jointly with civil society organizations.“The decisions governments make now will shape the future of work for millions of people,” Simet said. “They should ensure that platform work is governed by fair pay, safety, and social security, not exploitation.”
articles.content:
Click to expand Image Delivery workers driving through New Delhi, India, 2026. © 2026 Human Rights Watch

(New York) – Gig workers around the world experience long hours, unpredictable and declining pay, and serious safety risks, Human Rights Watch said in a report released today. Governments negotiating a landmark treaty under the International Labour Organization (ILO) on platform work in June 2026 should adopt strong, binding standards to ensure fair pay, safe working conditions, and access to social security for gig workers worldwide.

The multimedia report, “Algorithms of Exploitation: Rights Abuses in the Gig Economy and the Global Fight for Change,” documents the experiences of platform workers across nine countries, including India, Kenya, Kuwait, Lebanon, Mexico, Pakistan, Saudi Arabia, the UAE, and the United Kingdom. Human Rights Watch found that workers across all countries studied face low and unstable earnings, unsafe working conditions, and little or no protection when they are injured or unable to work.

“Platform companies have built a business model that sidesteps labor protections and shifts risks and costs onto the workers,” said Lena Simet, senior economic justice advisor at Human Rights Watch. “The ILO negotiations are the first global effort to get governments to course correct and ensure that using this model does not come at the expense of workers’ rights.”

Human Rights Watch interviewed drivers and delivery workers in India, Kenya, Lebanon, Mexico, Pakistan, and the UK as well as migrant returnees from Bangladesh and Nepal who previously worked for companies in Saudi Arabia, the UAE, and Kuwait to show examples of the human impact of unregulated work in the platform economy.

The ILO estimates that platform work nearly doubled between 2016 and 2021, while the World Bank estimates that up to 435 million people worldwide earn income through these labor platforms. Yet labor protections have not kept pace.

The convention under negotiation should address existing gaps in protection by including key guarantees for all platform workers, regardless of employment status, Human Rights Watch said. Because platform companies routinely classify workers as independent contractors or self-employed, this in many countries excludes them from minimum wage, social security, and occupational safety guarantees.

Workers described long hours, unpredictable and declining pay, and serious safety risks, often without social security or support if an injury or illness left them unable to work.

Apraham Orfalian, a driver in Beirut, said his earnings have declined steadily since 2015, leaving him unable to cover everyday expenses or contribute to social security. After his car and phone were stolen in a violent robbery while he was working for Uber, he was left without income and said he received no support from the company.

Agnes Mwongera, a driver in Nairobi, said she had been assaulted by a passenger and received no response when she reported the attack to her company.

Graeme Franes, a courier who used a bicycle to deliver food in Scotland, UK, said he was unable to work for six months after an attack left him with a broken arm. “I had to rely on friends and family,” he said. “That was a really tough time.”

By classifying gig workers as independent contractors, companies in many countries are able to avoid obligations for minimum wages, occupational safety, and social security. At the same time, they exercise significant control over workers through algorithmic systems that determine pay, assign tasks, and can suspend workers often without transparency or effective recourse.

Human Rights Watch previously found that after expenses, many platform workers in the United States earn well below a living wage and the statutory minimum wage. Workers in other countries reported similar dynamics, with earnings often insufficient to meet everyday expenses. This exploitative model allows companies to capture a growing share of revenue while shifting costs onto workers, contributing to widening inequality in labor markets.

Human Rights Watch urges governments to adopt strong standards that:

Establish a presumption of employment for workers when companies exercise control over them, to prevent misclassification;

Require fair pay, including compensation for all working time, and earnings that meet at least minimum wage or living wage standards;

Guarantee access to social security for all workers, including in cases of injury, illness, unemployment, and older age;

Mandate algorithmic transparency from platform companies, including information for workers on how their pay is calculated, how tasks and jobs are priced and assigned, and how incentive programs work;

Ensure accountability for platform companies, including accessible ways for workers to challenge automated decisions, including decisions to deactivate their accounts;

Extend occupational health and safety protections to all platform workers and require the companies to adopt safeguards against extreme heat and other dangerous working conditions; and

Guarantee workers’ rights to organize and bargain collectively without retaliation.



Human Rights Watch has contributed to the ILO process through submissions outlining rights-aligned approaches to regulating platform work, including proposals developed jointly with civil society organizations.

“The decisions governments make now will shape the future of work for millions of people,” Simet said. “They should ensure that platform work is governed by fair pay, safety, and social security, not exploitation.”
articles.reasoning: The article highlights a significant global effort led by the ILO to negotiate a landmark treaty aimed at protecting gig workers worldwide, ensuring fair pay, safe working conditions, and social security. This initiative addresses a broad and meaningful issue affecting hundreds of millions of workers globally, with detailed context on the challenges faced and proposed binding standards to improve labor rights and reduce exploitation.
articles.personality_title: New global treaty aims to protect gig workers’ rights and safety
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Gig workers are people who work through apps or online platforms, like delivery drivers or ride-hailing drivers. Many of these workers face problems like unpredictable pay, long hours, and unsafe conditions. So far, laws have not kept up with this growing type of work.

**What happened** – In June 2026, governments will negotiate a new treaty under the International Labour Organization (ILO) to protect gig workers worldwide. A report by Human Rights Watch shared stories from workers in nine countries, revealing low pay, unsafe work, and little support if they get hurt. The treaty aims to set clear rules for fair pay, safety, and social security benefits for all platform workers.

**Impact** – This treaty is important because it is the first global effort to make sure gig workers get the same protections as regular employees, even though many companies call them independent contractors. Without these protections, workers can lose income, health support, or safety guarantees. The treaty could change how millions of people who rely on platform work are treated, making their jobs safer and fairer.

**What’s next step** – Governments will discuss and hopefully agree on strong, binding rules during the ILO meeting. These rules include fair wages, social security, safety measures, and transparency about how companies use algorithms to decide pay and tasks. If successful, this treaty will guide countries to improve laws and protect gig workers worldwide.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new global treaty being negotiated aims to ensure gig workers receive fair pay, safe working conditions, and social protections, marking a major step forward for millions of platform workers.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un nouveau traité mondial vise à protéger les droits et la sécurité des travailleurs de la gig economy
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les travailleurs de la gig economy sont ceux qui travaillent via des applications ou des plateformes en ligne, comme les livreurs ou chauffeurs. Beaucoup rencontrent des problèmes comme des salaires instables, de longues heures et des conditions dangereuses. Jusqu’à présent, les lois n’ont pas suivi cette forme de travail en plein essor.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En juin 2026, les gouvernements négocieront un nouveau traité sous l’Organisation Internationale du Travail (OIT) pour protéger les travailleurs de la gig economy dans le monde entier. Un rapport de Human Rights Watch a partagé des témoignages de travailleurs dans neuf pays, révélant des salaires bas, un travail dangereux et peu de soutien en cas de blessure. Ce traité vise à fixer des règles claires pour un salaire équitable, la sécurité et la protection sociale pour tous les travailleurs des plateformes.

**Impact** – Ce traité est important parce qu’il s’agit du premier effort mondial pour garantir que les travailleurs de la gig economy bénéficient des mêmes protections que les employés classiques, même si beaucoup sont considérés comme travailleurs indépendants. Sans ces protections, les travailleurs risquent de perdre leurs revenus, leur soutien santé ou leurs garanties de sécurité. Ce traité pourrait changer la vie de millions de personnes qui dépendent du travail sur plateforme, en rendant leur emploi plus sûr et plus juste.

**Prochaine étape** – Les gouvernements discuteront et espèrent adopter des règles fortes et contraignantes lors de la réunion de l’OIT. Ces règles incluent des salaires équitables, la sécurité sociale, des mesures de sécurité, et la transparence sur la façon dont les entreprises utilisent des algorithmes pour fixer les salaires et les tâches. Si le traité est adopté, il guidera les pays à améliorer les lois et à protéger les travailleurs de la gig economy dans le monde.

**En une phrase** – Un nouveau traité mondial en négociation vise à garantir un salaire équitable, des conditions de travail sûres et une protection sociale aux travailleurs de la gig economy, une avancée majeure pour des millions de travailleurs.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo tratado mundial busca proteger los derechos y la seguridad de los trabajadores de plataformas digitales
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los trabajadores de plataformas digitales son personas que trabajan a través de aplicaciones, como conductores o repartidores. Muchos enfrentan problemas como pagos impredecibles, largas horas y condiciones inseguras. Hasta ahora, las leyes no han avanzado al ritmo de este tipo de trabajo en crecimiento.

**Qué pasó** – En junio de 2026, los gobiernos negociarán un nuevo tratado bajo la Organización Internacional del Trabajo (OIT) para proteger a los trabajadores de plataformas en todo el mundo. Un informe de Human Rights Watch contó historias de trabajadores en nueve países, mostrando bajos salarios, trabajo inseguro y poca ayuda si se lesionan. El tratado busca establecer reglas claras para un pago justo, seguridad y acceso a la seguridad social para todos los trabajadores de plataformas.

**Impacto** – Este tratado es importante porque es el primer esfuerzo global para asegurar que los trabajadores de plataformas tengan las mismas protecciones que los empleados regulares, aunque muchas empresas los clasifiquen como contratistas independientes. Sin estas protecciones, los trabajadores pueden perder ingresos, apoyo médico o garantías de seguridad. El tratado podría cambiar la vida de millones de personas que dependen del trabajo en plataformas, haciendo sus empleos más seguros y justos.

**Próximo paso** – Los gobiernos discutirán y esperan acordar reglas fuertes y obligatorias en la reunión de la OIT. Estas reglas incluyen pago justo, seguridad social, medidas de seguridad y transparencia sobre cómo las empresas usan algoritmos para decidir pagos y tareas. Si se aprueba, el tratado guiará a los países para mejorar las leyes y proteger a los trabajadores de plataformas globalmente.

**Resumen en una frase** – Un nuevo tratado mundial en negociación busca garantizar pago justo, condiciones seguras y protección social para los trabajadores de plataformas, un avance clave para millones.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of a diverse group of delivery vehicles—bicycles, motorcycles, and small cars—traveling together along a winding city road at sunset, their paths illuminated by warm, soft light; the vehicles cast long, connected shadows symbolizing unity and protection, set against a backdrop of stylized cityscape silhouettes representing global urban centers.
